ops.title: TUI framework evaluation for cco tui (2026-07): constrained → Bubble Tea v2; unconstrained → Ratatui

ops.body:
Nine-agent research pass (mid-2026 state) over TUI frameworks for a `cco tui` front-end, followed by a three-judge first-principles panel with constraints removed. Two answers depending on constraints; pending user decision.

## Answer 1 — constrained (Go ecosystem, existing GoReleaser pipeline, reuse wire clients): Bubble Tea v2
With the constraints in force, Bubble Tea v2 + Charm stack (charm.land/bubbletea/v2, bubbles/v2, lipgloss/v2, glamour/v2) wins decisively:
- A Go TUI reuses `daemon.Client` (unix-socket RPC) + `consume.ConsumeEvents` (SSE resume/cursor/backoff) verbatim — `cco agent watch` (orchestrate/cli.go streamAgent/watchAllAgents) is the exact pattern. A non-Go TUI must reverse-engineer an unversioned wire contract (no OpenAPI; ops/payloads are unexported Go structs), implement the unix-socket bootstrap (pure-HTTP client impossible — SSE port/subject_id only via OpResolve/OpStatus), and hand-sync every payload struct. Raw transcript bytes never cross the wire (only derived orchestrate.status/inbound events).
- v2 GA 2026-02-23 (Cursed Renderer/ultraviolet), dogfooded in Crush; category converged on it (Claude Squad, Gas Town, amux, gh-dash).
- OpenTUI disqualified here: no Go embedding (Go bindings unmaintained), second Bun+Zig toolchain, 50–136MB artifact.

## Answer 2 — unconstrained first principles (language/pipeline free, daemon API can change): Ratatui
Judge panel (fable ×3, lenses: product-velocity / longevity-3yr / architecture-perf) split 1-1-1 but Ratatui won two lenses and the aggregate (23.3 vs BT 21.3, OpenTUI 17.8, Textual 17.4):
- **Architecture (9/10, winner)**: only candidate whose frame cost is provably bounded by screen area, independent of stream count/rate — immediate-mode buffer rebuild + cell diff + a tokio frame clock coalescing N token streams. No GC/interpreter jitter. TestBackend+insta is the only stable in-core render-testing contract in the field.
- **Longevity (8.5/10, winner)**: the only option that already survived its sponsor-failure event (tui-rs abandonment → community fork), multi-maintainer, 1,296 reverse deps, modular v0.30 workspace = cheapest fork escape hatch (yazi already thrives on a private fork).
- **Costs**: it's a rendering library, not an app framework — you own the app loop, event fan-in, markdown/highlight caching; textarea/markdown/tree are third-party crates to vet. Slowest velocity lane (5.8/10).
- **OpenTUI**: won product-velocity (8.3 — in-box Markdown/tree-sitter Code/Diff widgets ARE this app's core screen; Solid signals = O(changed node) token appends) but scored 2/10 on longevity: 0.x weekly breaking releases, markdown internals mid-rewrite (MarkTree), roadmap literally "find what OpenCode and terminal.shop need", maintainer on record "still some time away from production ready" (2026-04), two bindings abandoned in year one, worst fork escape hatch (TS+Zig on pre-1.0 Zig). AI-authored code rots fastest against a churning 0.x API.
- **Bubble Tea unconstrained**: everyone's runner-up (6.8/7.5/7) — untagged ultraviolet core, no flexbox, no tree widget, teatest in x/exp; wins back instantly if any code-sharing constraint returns.

## Decisive experiments (from panel mind-changers)
- Head-to-head spike: same 4-pane streaming-transcript prototype in Ratatui vs OpenTUI, instrumented for frame time/CPU/RSS.
- Product question that collapses the perf differentiation: if only 1–2 transcript panes are ever visible (attach-style like Claude Squad), ranking re-sorts on ergonomics/content widgets, favoring OpenTUI/BT.
- Charm tagging ultraviolet + graduating teatest would lift BT to near-tie on architecture.

## Cross-cutting gotchas (any Go path)
Pin ultraviolet pseudo-version in lockstep with bubbletea/v2 (silent compositor breakage — amux warning); cc-orchestrate pins cc-interact v0.1.3 vs sibling HEAD v0.7.0+ (auth/caught-up/Inject only at HEAD); `ncruces/go-sqlite3` (WASM, cgo-free) is the reference for SQLite in GoReleaser pipelines; serious dashboards routinely end up forking their framework (k9s/lazygit/yazi).
